direction.title: Decentralized feedback-driven discovery with adaptive formal verification
direction.rationale: Multiple branches (A–F) have recently taken actions to decentralize conjecture generation and introduce feedback loops (structural changes from single-agent to multi-agent, reasoning_pattern from static to feedback-driven). The collective trajectory is toward fully autonomous, multi-agent discovery. However, the residual gap of fixed formal verification remains. The implied next problem after this trajectory completes is how to make the verification mechanism itself adaptive to new formal systems—so that the decentralized feedback loop can operate across diverse mathematical domains.
direction.trend_endpoint: A fully autonomous multi-agent system that generates and verifies conjectures via collective learning
direction.implied_next_problem: Enabling the verification component to adapt to arbitrary formal systems without human intervention, overcoming the fixed-environment assumption inherited from all ancestors.
direction.non_obvious_property: Property: Co-adaptation of conjecture generation and verification mechanisms across formal systems. Obvious composition that fails: Multi-agent RL + automated theorem prover (e.g., E prover, Vampire). This fails because the prover is still domain-specific (e.g., first-order logic) and does not learn to adapt its own inference rules. Why prior work has not formalized this property: Prior work externalizes verification to fixed tools, treating them as non-learnable oracles.

per_trend_predictions.direction: Develop a decentralized verification ensemble that adapts to arbitrary formal systems via cooperative multi-agent learning, replacing the fixed verifier assumed in prior work.
per_trend_predictions.rationale: The trend endpoint achieves autonomous conjecture generation and verification within one formal system, but inherits the assumption of a fixed environment. To generalize to arbitrary formal systems—a necessary step for full autonomy—we need a verification mechanism that can adapt without human intervention. Recent advances in multi-agent RL and ensemble learning make this feasible.
per_trend_predictions.builds_on: Decentralized feedback-driven discovery with adaptive formal verification
per_trend_predictions.persistent_gap: The verification component cannot adapt to new formal systems; it is tied to a specific logic or proof assistant (e.g., Lean). This limits deployment in novel mathematical domains.
per_trend_predictions.why_still_open: Prior work on adaptive verification either assumes a fixed meta-language (e.g., logical frameworks) or requires human-engineered rule sets. No existing method provides an autonomous mechanism that can infer and evolve verification rules from agent interactions alone.
per_trend_predictions.bridge_keep: The multi-agent decentralized architecture for conjecture generation and feedback-driven learning.
per_trend_predictions.bridge_remove: The assumption of a fixed formal system (e.g., a specific proof assistant or logic) for verification.
per_trend_predictions.bridge_add: Introduce an ensemble of verification probes – small neural networks each specialized in checking a local proof property (e.g., rule application, variable scoping). Probes are trained via cooperative multi-agent RL: reward is global acceptance of conjectures by all probes, and probes are added/removed dynamically. Weights are updated via consensus-based gradient aggregation, and the ensemble maintains a calibration property: the average probe score is a consistent estimator of validity across any formal system because probes are trained to be conditionally independent given proof correctness.
per_trend_predictions.non_obvious_property: Property: The ensemble achieves cross-system calibration—the average of probe scores remains a consistent estimator of proof validity even when the underlying formal system changes. Why prior work cannot exploit it: Neural verifiers (e.g., 'DeepHOL', 'GPT-f') are trained for a specific logic and lose calibration when applied to a different formal system; multi-agent RL for verification (e.g., 'MathAgent') does not provide any calibration guarantee. A composition like 'neural verifier + MARL' fails because no component ensures that the ensemble score generalizes across systems—the calibration emerges only from the specific conditional independence training and consensus aggregation, which are absent in standard approaches.
per_trend_predictions.obvious_composition_check: The shortest composition description is 'multi-agent RL + ensemble of neural verifiers'. This is materially wrong because it omits the load-bearing calibration mechanism (conditional independence training + consensus aggregation) that guarantees cross-system validity. Without that mechanism, an ensemble of verifiers does not adapt to new formal systems.
per_trend_predictions.forest_implied_next_problem: Enabling the verification component to adapt to arbitrary formal systems without human intervention, overcoming the fixed-environment assumption inherited from all ancestors.
